From discrete to competitive: the symmetric-quadratic contest

All-pay contest with dynamic scoring — round-(T−1) equilibria σ(m,n) and their limit. Setting: identical valuations, no initial lead, quadratic effort cost.

Parameters. $v_1=v_2=1$, initial lead $\Delta=0$, cost $c(e)=e^2$, reach $w=c^{-1}(1)=1$. Player 1 mixes over $m$ atoms $x_1<\dots

1. The competitive equilibrium (the target)

For quadratic cost the round-(T−1) game has a symmetric continuous equilibrium in which both players earn zero — full rent dissipation:

$$G(x)=e^{x}-1 \quad\text{on } [0,\ln 2],\qquad g(x)=e^{x},\qquad \bar x=\ln 2\approx 0.6931 .$$

The maximum support $\bar x=\ln 2$ (from $G(\bar x)=1$) is our yardstick: if the discrete σ(m,n) converge to this competitive equilibrium, their top atoms $x_m,\,y_n$ must climb to $\ln 2$ and their CDFs must approach $G$.

2. The discrete family σ(m,n), in closed recursion

With $g_\ell=1+y_\ell$, $h_k=1+x_k$, $Q_k=\sum_{\ell\le k}q_\ell$, $P_k=\sum_{j\le k}p_j$, the first-order conditions locate the atoms,

$$x_k=\frac{\sum_{\ell\le k} q_\ell\, g_\ell}{1+Q_k},\qquad y_\ell=\frac{\sum_{k<\ell} p_k\, h_k}{1+P_{\ell-1}},$$

and the indifference conditions, after substituting the FOC, collapse to the value identity $u_1=Q_k-\sum_{\ell\le k}q_\ell g_\ell^2+(1+Q_k)x_k^2$ (independent of $k$). Differencing successive levels gives each new mass explicitly — no Gröbner basis:

$$q_{j+1}=(1+Q_j)\big[(1+y_{j+1}-x_j)^2-1\big],\qquad p_j=(1+P_{j-1})\big[(1+x_j-y_j)^2-1\big].$$

At $k=1$ ($y_1=0$) the identity gives the closed-form payoffs $\;u_1=\dfrac{q_1^2}{1+q_1},\quad u_2=0.$ The highest atom's mass never enters payoffs, so it is the leftover fixed by $\sum\text{mass}=1$ — a single monotone root-find on $q_1$ closes the system.

3. Step-CDFs vs the competitive equilibrium

Player 2 carries the boundary atom at $0$ (mass $q_1$); player 1 has no mass at $0$. Both staircases straddle $G(x)=e^x-1$ and tighten around it as the supports grow.

4. Convergence of the maximum support to ln 2

Half-integer abscissae mark the $(m,m{+}1)$ rungs. In that rung player 2's top atom $y_{m+1}$ descends to $\ln 2$; in the square rung $(m,m)$ player 1's top atom $x_m$ climbs to it. Both edges converge to $\ln 2$.

5. Quantitative convergence: the sup-norm $\lVert F_m-G\rVert_\infty$

The maximum support is one coordinate; the Kolmogorov distance $D_m=\sup_x\lvert F_m(x)-G(x)\rvert$ measures the whole distribution. For a step CDF against the continuous $G$ the supremum is attained at a jump, so $D_m$ is computed exactly. Both players' distances fall monotonically toward $0$ (log scale below) — quantitative evidence that σ(m,n) converges in distribution to the competitive equilibrium, not merely in maximum support.

Player 2's distance is bounded below by its boundary atom $q_1$ (the jump at $0$, where $G(0)=0$); since $q_1\to 0$ this obstruction vanishes. The slope on the log plot indicates the rate (≈ $O(1/m)$).

6. The chain

(m,n)$u_1$$u_2$$q_1$$x_m$$y_n$$\ln 2-x_m$$D_m^{(1)}$$D_m^{(2)}$

Computed by the self-contained Julia recursion in Julia/converge/ (solver.jl), verified against the project's algebraic-geometry results through (5,6).
